solution_text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Conversion of Chlorobenzene to Product X}}$

Chlorobenzene is treated with aqueous sodium hydroxide at a high temperature (623 K) and high pressure (300 atm), followed by acidification. This industrial process is known as the **Dow Process**. The nucleophilic substitution of the chlorine atom by a hydroxyl group occurs under these drastic conditions to yield phenol.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{Cl} \xrightarrow[\text{623 K, 300 atm}]{\text{NaOH}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{ONa} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH (Phenol)}$$

$\rightarrow$ $\textbf{Product X}$ is $\textbf{ Phenol}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Oxidation of Product X to Product Y}}$

Phenol ([\textbf{X}]) is then subjected to oxidation using sodium dichromate ($\text{Na}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$) in the presence of sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$). This reaction involves the oxidation of the aromatic ring to produce a conjugated diketone.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Na}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7 / \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{O=C}_6\text{H}_4\text{=O (}\textit{p}\text{-Benzoquinone)}$$

$\rightarrow$ $\textbf{Product Y}$ is $\textit{p}$-Benzoquinone.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The products are phenol and $\textit{p}$-benzoquinone, respectively.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{X = Phenol, Y = } \textit{p}\text{-Benzoquinone}}.$

$\textbf{Understanding the Transformation:}$

Tetrabrominated alkane → Intermediate B → Alkyne

$\textbf{Step 1 - Reagent A:}$

• NaI/acetone performs dehalogenation
• I⁻ replaces Br atoms via nucleophilic substitution
• Forms intermediate (dibromide or alkene)

$\textbf{Step 2 - Reagent C:}$

• NaNH₂ (strong base) eliminates HBr → alkyne
• CH₃I alkylates terminal alkyne via acetylide ion

$\textbf{Evaluation of Options:}$

$\textbf{Option A:}$ Zn/CH₃OH then KOH/ethanol - Not ideal for this transformation ✗

$\textbf{Option B:}$ Dilute NaOH then Br₂/CHCl₃ - Adds bromine, doesn't form alkyne ✗

$\textbf{Option C:}$ Conc. NaOH then HBr/acetic acid - HBr adds to alkyne (wrong direction) ✗

$\textbf{Option D:}$ NaI/acetone then NaNH₂/CH₃I - Correct sequence ✓

$\textbf{Conclusion:}$
A = NaI/acetone (dehalogenation), C = NaNH₂/CH₃I (elimination + alkylation)

Therefore, the correct answer is $\textbf{Option D}$.

1. Electrophillic Addition occurs at most stable carbocation.
2.Hydrogenation reduces the remaining 2 pie bonds
3.Alcoholic KOH further removes Br by E2 elimination to form most stable Alkene

$\pmb{\text{Step-by-step reaction analysis:}}$

$\pmb{\text{1. Conversion of } C_{7}H_{8} \text{ to A:}}$
The starting material $C_{7}H_{8}$ is $\textbf{Toluene}$ ($C_{6}H_{5}CH_{3}$). Reaction with $3$ moles of $Cl_{2}$ under heating ($\Delta$) favors free-radical side-chain chlorination over electrophilic aromatic substitution.
$$C_{6}H_{5}CH_{3} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{3Cl_{2}} C_{6}H_{5}CCl_{3} + 3HCl$$
Product $\pmb{\text{A}}$ is $\textbf{Benzotrichloride}$.

$\pmb{\text{2. Conversion of A to B:}}$
In the presence of $Fe$ (which forms $FeBr_{3}$ in situ), electrophilic aromatic substitution occurs. The $-CCl_{3}$ group is a strongly deactivating group and is $\textbf{meta-directing}$ because it withdraws electron density from the ring via the $-I$ effect and reverse hyperconjugation.
$$C_{6}H_{5}CCl_{3} \xrightarrow{Br_{2}/Fe} m\text{-Br-}C_{6}H_{4}CCl_{3}$$
Product $\pmb{\text{B}}$ is $\textbf{m-bromobenzotrichloride}$.

$\pmb{\text{3. Conversion of B to C:}}$
Treatment with $Zn/HCl$ (a reducing agent) reduces the trichloromethyl group ($-CCl_{3}$) back to a methyl group ($-CH_{3}$).
$$m\text{-Br-}C_{6}H_{4}CCl_{3} \xrightarrow{Zn/HCl} m\text{-Br-}C_{6}H_{4}CH_{3}$$
Product $\pmb{\text{C}}$ is $\textbf{m-bromotoluene}$.



$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The sequence results in the formation of $\textbf{m-bromotoluene}$.

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Reaction Pathway:}}$
The reaction of $m$-bromoanisole with $\text{NaNH}_2$ in liquid $\text{NH}_3$ proceeds via a $\pmb{\text{benzyne (elimination-addition)}}$ mechanism.

$\pmb{\text{Mechanism:}}$
1. $\pmb{\text{Elimination:}}$ Strong base $\text{NH}_2^-$ removes an ortho-proton followed by $\text{Br}^-$ loss to form a benzyne intermediate.
2. $\pmb{\text{Addition:}}$ Nucleophilic attack of $\text{NH}_2^-$ on the benzyne triple bond.

$\pmb{\text{Regioselectivity:}}$
The $-\text{OCH}_3$ group exerts a $\pmb{\text{-I effect}}$, stabilizing the carbanion intermediate when the nucleophile attacks the $\pmb{\text{meta}}$ position. 

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The major product is $\pmb{\text{m-anisidine}}$. Since the process involve loss of $\text{HBr}$ followed by addition of $\text{NH}_3$, it is an $\pmb{\text{Elimination-Addition reaction}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Mechanistic Analysis:}}$

$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Reaction} & \textbf{Reagent Type} & \textbf{Mechanism} \\ \hline (i) & \text{Weak Nucleophile (Ethanol)} & S_{N}2 \\ \hline (ii) & \text{Strong Nucleophile (Ethoxide)} & S_{N}2 \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Explanation:}}$

  - In both reactions, the substrate is $\textbf{isobutyl bromide}$, which is a primary alkyl halide.
  - Primary alkyl halides are highly hindered toward $S_{N}1$ mechanisms because the resulting primary carbocation is very unstable.
  - If an $S_{N}1$ mechanism were to occur, the primary carbocation $(CH_{3})_{2}CH-CH_{2}^{+}$ would undergo a $\textbf{1,2-hydride shift}$ to form a more stable tertiary carbocation, leading to a rearranged product.
  - 
  - Since the products shown in both (i) and (ii) are $\textbf{unrearranged}$ (simple substitution of the bromide with the ethoxy group), we can conclude that no carbocation intermediate was formed.
  - This indicates that both reactions proceed via a concerted $\textbf{S}_{\textbf{N}}\textbf{2}$ pathway where the nucleophile attacks while the leaving group departs.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Both reactions follow the $S_{N}2$ mechanism as there is no rearrangement observed.

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Theoretical Analysis:}}$

The $S_N2$ (Substitution Nucleophilic Bimolecular) mechanism proceeds via a backside attack of the nucleophile on the carbon atom bonded to the leaving group. The reaction rate is primarily governed by $\textbf{steric hindrance}$ at the reaction center.

$\pmb{\text{Comparison of Substrates:}}$

- $\textbf{Option B (Ethyl Bromide):}$ $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br}$ is a primary ($1^\circ$) alkyl halide with minimal steric bulk, allowing for the fastest nucleophilic approach.

- $\textbf{Option C (n-Propyl Bromide):}$ $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Br}$ is also $1^\circ$, but the slightly longer chain increases the steric footprint compared to the ethyl group.

- $\textbf{Option A (Isobutyl Bromide):}$ $\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{CHCH}_2\text{Br}$ is a $1^\circ$ halide with a branch at the $\beta$-carbon, which significantly slows down the reaction.

- $\textbf{Option D (Neopentyl Bromide):}$ $\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_3\text{CCH}_2\text{Br}$ is extremely hindered due to three methyl groups on the $\beta$-carbon, making $S_N2$ nearly impossible.

$\pmb{\text{Reactivity Order (Array Representation):}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Alkyl Group} & \textbf{Relative Rate Preference} \\ \hline \text{Methyl} & \text{Highest} \\ \hline \text{Ethyl (B)} & \text{High} \\ \hline \text{n-Propyl (C)} & \text{Moderate} \\ \hline \text{Isobutyl (A)} & \text{Low} \\ \hline \text{Neopentyl (D)} & \text{Lowest} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Therefore, $\textbf{Ethyl bromide}$ has the highest relative rate among the given choices.

Correct Answer: $\boxed{B}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Conversion of Ethoxybenzene to Product P}}$

In the nitration of ethoxybenzene, the ethoxy group ($-\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5$) acts as an activating and $\textit{ortho/para}$ directing group due to the $+M$ effect of the oxygen lone pair. Because the ethoxy group is sterically bulky, the electrophilic substitution by the nitronium ion ($\text{NO}_2^+$) occurs primarily at the $\textit{para}$ position to minimize steric strain.
$$\rightarrow \textbf{Product P: } \text{1-ethoxy-4-nitrobenzene}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Conversion of Product P to Product Q}}$

Product $\textbf{P}$ is then subjected to electrophilic aromatic bromination with two equivalents of $\text{Br}_2$. We must determine the directing effects of the two substituents present:
$\bullet$ The ethoxy group at $\text{C}_1$ is $\textit{ortho}$ directing (positions 2 and 6).
$\bullet$ The nitro group at $\text{C}_4$ is a $\textit{meta}$ director (positions 2 and 6).

Both groups reinforce each other, directing the bromine atoms to positions 2 and 6. Since two equivalents of bromine are used, both available positions are substituted.
$$\rightarrow \textbf{Product Q: } \text{2,6-dibromo-1-ethoxy-4-nitrobenzene}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determination of the Ratio } \alpha}$

$\bullet$ **Oxygen Atoms in Q:** There is 1 oxygen atom in the ethoxy group and 2 oxygen atoms in the nitro group. Total O atoms = 3.
$\bullet$ **Bromine Atoms in Q:** There are 2 bromine atoms substituted on the benzene ring. Total Br atoms = 2.

The ratio $\alpha$ is defined as:
$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Number of O atoms}}{\text{Number of Br atoms}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

$\pmb{\text{Final Calculation:}}$

The required value is $20\alpha$:
$$20 \times 1.5 = 30$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{30}.$
